questions.text: A crystal system having all sides (a, b and c) unequal and angles α = β = γ = 90 is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is incorrect because a cubic system has all sides equal (a = b = c) and all angles at 90°.
This is incorrect because in a rhombohedral system, all sides are equal (a = b = c), but the angles are not necessarily 90°.
This is correct because an orthorhombic system has all three sides unequal (a ≠ b ≠ c), but all angles are 90° (α = β = γ = 90°).
This is incorrect because a hexagonal system has two equal sides (a = b ≠ c) and angles α = β = 90°, but γ = 120°.

questions.explanation: This is correct because an orthorhombic system has all three sides unequal (a ≠ b ≠ c), but all angles are 90° (α = β = γ = 90°).

questions.text: Which of the following solids is an example of a substance with a macromolecular structure?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Aluminum chloride forms a covalent bond because the electronegativity difference between Al3+ and Cl- is small. The tendency of aluminum (less electropositive) to lose an electron to form cation and chlorine (less electronegative) to accept an electron to form an anion is less in this case.
Ice is a hydrogen bonded molecular solid. Hydrogen atom of one water molecule forms hydrogen bonds with the oxygen atom of the neighbouring water molecule.
The chemical bond between magnesium and oxygen in magnesium oxide is ionic in nature. This is a consequence of the large difference in the electronegativities of magnesium and oxygen.

In silicon dioxide, each silicon atom bond is covalently bonded to four oxygen atoms, and each oxygen atom bonds covalently to two silicon atoms in a giant molecular/macromolecular structure. If you refer back to the examples on silicon, you can remind yourself of the reason that SiO2 is macromolecular. Silicon is reluctant to form multiple bonds, so discrete O=Si=O molecules, analogous to O=C=O, do not occur. To satisfy silicon’s valence of 4 and oxygen’s valence of 2, each silicon must be surrounded by four oxygens and each oxygen by two silicons. This can be represented schematically by the Lewis diagram.
Ionic compounds, such as sodium chloride (NaCl), are formed by a transfer of electrons that creates ions. Ions exert electrostatic force on each other, which forms ionic bonds.

questions.explanation: SiO2 has a macromolecular structure only among the compounds above. While other compounds possess a simple molecular structure, hence D is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Molecular crystals are substances that have relatively weak intermolecular binding, such as dry ice (solidified carbon dioxide), solid forms of the noble gases (e.g., argon, krypton, and xenon), and crystals of numerous organic compounds

questions.text: Which one is not related to evaporation?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This term refers to a process that happens without interruption. Evaporation can be continuous, but it is not a defining characteristic of the process itself.
Evaporation is indeed an endothermic process, meaning it absorbs heat from the surroundings. This is a key feature of evaporation.
This term describes a process that releases heat. Since evaporation absorbs heat, it is not exothermic, making it the correct answer to the question.

This term means that a process can occur on its own without external influence. Evaporation can be spontaneous under certain conditions, but it is not inherently linked to the definition of evaporation.

questions.explanation: Evaporation is a continuous and spontaneous process. It requires absorption of heat from surroundings, so it is endothermic. Therefore, exothermic is not related to evaporation

options.text: Temperature has no effect on freely moving molecules
Colliding capability of molecule will become lower

questions.explanation: According to Charles's law, the volume occupied by a fixed amount of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature, if the pressure remains constant. Since the piston is movable, hence increased temperature causes the volume to be increased.

questions.text: Solid NaCl is a bad conductor of electricity because:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: NaCl is an ionic salt

options.text: In the solid state, there are no ions
options.explanation: This statement is wrong because solid NaCl has charged particles i.e. ions namely Na+ and Cl−

options.text: In solid NaCl, there is no migration of ions
options.explanation: In solids, NaCl ions are bound by strong electrostatic forces. Free ions participate in good electric conduction. When NaCl is dissolved in water, ions are capable of moving and conducting electricity. A substance can only conduct electricity if it contains charged particles (electrons or ions) that are free to move around. In solid sodium chloride, there are ions but these ions are locked into the ionic lattice and are unable to move.NaCl (common salt) is solid in state and solid ions or compounds don't conduct electricity. It needs to be either melted, molten, or dissolved in a solution (i.e. water) first. Only then will the electrons be free to move to either the Cathode (-ve) or to the Anode (+ve). In a liquid state, the electrons are free enough to move. Hence, solid NaCl is a bad conductor of electricity because its ions cannot move freely.

options.text: In solid NaCl, there are not electron
options.explanation: In NaCl, the conductance is due to the ions and not the flow of electrons

questions.explanation: Conduction of electricity occurs when there is a free-moving charge (electrons or ions), so the answer is C.

questions.text: All the collisions between the particles of gases are elastic in nature. What is meant by "Elastic Collisions"?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: No change in the total kinetic energy
options.explanation: This option correctly defines elastic collisions, where the total kinetic energy before and after the collision remains constant. Therefore, option A is the correct answer.

options.text: The velocity of the molecules changes
options.explanation: While the velocity of individual molecules may change during a collision, this does not define an elastic collision. In elastic collisions, the total kinetic energy remains the same, so this statement is misleading.

options.text: No change in potential energy during the collisions
options.explanation: This statement is not relevant to elastic collisions, as potential energy is not typically a focus in the context of elastic collisions, which are defined solely by kinetic energy considerations.

options.text: No change in mass during the collisions
options.explanation: Though mass remains constant during elastic collisions, this characteristic applies to all types of collisions (elastic and inelastic) and does not specifically define the nature of elastic collisions.

questions.explanation: In elastic collisions, the total kinetic energy before and after collision remains the same. Gas molecules may change direction and speed, but energy is conserved. This assumption is a key part of the kinetic molecular theory of gases.

questions.text: Water has maximum density at:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At -4 C, water would be in a solid state (ice), which is less dense than liquid water.
At -1 C, water is close to freezing, and its density decreases as it approaches the solid state.
At 0 C, water begins to form ice, which is less dense due to the crystal structure of ice.
At 4 C, water reaches its maximum density because the water molecules are most closely packed.

questions.explanation: Water exhibits an anomalous behavior where its density increases as it cools until it reaches 4 C, where it is most dense. Below 4 C, water begins to expand again as it approaches the freezing point, due to the formation of a crystalline structure in ice. Therefore, the maximum density of water occurs at 4 C. The other options represent temperatures where water is either starting to freeze (-1 C, 0 C) or is already frozen (-4 C), both of which result in a lower density than at 4 C.

questions.text: The unit cell parameters of the monoclinic system are:

options.explanation: This describes a tetragonal system where two axes are equal, and all angles are right angles.
This correctly describes the monoclinic system, where all axes are unequal, and only two angles are right angles.
This represents an orthorhombic system, not hexagonal, where all angles are right angles but sides are unequal.
This describes a cubic system where all sides and angles are equal.

questions.explanation: The monoclinic crystal system is characterized by three unequal axes, with two of them being perpendicular (α=γ=90°) and the third angle (β) differing from 90°. This distinguishes the monoclinic system from others where either all axes are equal or all angles are right angles. Option B correctly represents this with a≠b≠c ; α=γ=90° ; β≠90°. Options A, C, and D describe tetragonal, orthorhombic, and cubic systems, respectively, which do not fit the monoclinic parameters.

Under these conditions, the gas molecules are close to each other and they exert significant intermolecular forces on each other. Also, the volume of gas molecules becomes a significant portion of the total volume and cannot be neglected. Hence, the correct option is D.

Also, the volume of gas molecules becomes a significant portion of the total volume and cannot be neglected. Hence, the correct option is D.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true for Amorphous solids?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Amorphous solids lack symmetry because their atomic arrangement is irregular, unlike crystalline solids which have a symmetrical, orderly pattern.
Amorphous solids are isotropic, meaning they have uniform properties in all directions due to their irregular atomic arrangement, lacking long-range order.
Anisotropic materials, like crystalline solids, have direction-dependent properties, which is not the case for amorphous solids.

options.text: They cleave along a particular direction
options.explanation: Cleavage along specific directions is characteristic of crystalline solids due to their ordered structure. Amorphous solids break irregularly because of their random atomic arrangement.
Amorphous solids do not have a definite shape or form since their atoms are not arranged in a fixed, repeating pattern.

questions.explanation: Amorphous solids are isotropic because their lack of long-range order results in uniform physical properties in all directions. This is in contrast to crystalline solids, which are anisotropic due to their orderly atomic structure. Amorphous solids do not possess symmetry and cannot cleave along particular planes because their particles are randomly arranged. Additionally, they do not exhibit a definite shape as their molecular structure does not form a regular lattice.

questions.text: A child's balloon has a volume 3.80 dm3, when the temperature is 35°C. If the balloon is put in a refrigerator and cooled to 5°C, the approximate volume of the balloon is (assume pressure inside the balloon is equal to atmospheric pressure):
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option underestimates the effect of temperature change on the volume.
This is the correct answer. It uses Charles's Law to find the new volume when the temperature changes.
This option miscalculates the volume using incorrect temperature calculations.
This option slightly underestimates the volume, not accounting properly for the temperature decrease.
This option is far too low and does not apply Charles's Law correctly.

questions.explanation: To solve this, we apply Charles's Law, which states that at constant pressure, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its temperature in Kelvin. Start by converting the given temperatures from Celsius to Kelvin: T1 = 35°C + 273 = 308 K and T2 = 5°C + 273 = 278 K. Using the formula V1/T1 = V2/T2, we have:3.80 dm3 / 308 K = V2 / 278 K.Solving for V2 gives:V2 = (3.80 dm3 * 278 K) / 308 K = 3.43 dm3.Thus, the correct answer is 3.43 dm3. The other options either miscalculate the proportional change or do not apply Charles's Law correctly.

questions.text: If the matter in a given system at a given condition is divided into two equal parts, then the value of the extensive properties will become:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Extensive properties depend on the amount of matter, so dividing the matter would reduce the value, not increase it.
Correct. Extensive properties, such as mass or volume, are directly proportional to the amount of substance, so dividing the matter into two equal parts will halve these properties' values.

options.text: Remain the same as the original value
options.explanation: This would apply to intensive properties, which do not depend on the amount of matter, such as density or temperature.
Dividing the matter into two equal parts would result in half the value of the extensive property, not one-fourth.
Heat of formation is unrelated to the division of matter into parts. It's a specific property related to the formation of compounds.

questions.explanation: Extensive properties in thermodynamics, such as mass, volume, and energy, directly depend on the quantity of matter present. When the matter is divided into two equal parts, each part retains half of the original extensive property value. Intensive properties, on the other hand, such as temperature and density, do not change with the amount of matter and remain constant. Thus, the correct answer is that the value of extensive properties will become half of the original value. Option A is incorrect because doubling would imply an increase, which doesn't happen upon division. Option C is incorrect because it applies to intensive properties, not extensive ones. Option D is incorrect because dividing into two equal parts results in half, not one-fourth. Option E is irrelevant as it refers to a specific thermodynamic property unrelated to the division of matter.

questions.text: Comparative rates of diffusion of He and SO2 will be _.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This value results from an incorrect calculation of the square root of the molar mass ratio.
This is not the correct result of applying Graham's law to the given molar masses.

Correct! According to Graham's law of effusion, the rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the square root of the molar mass. For SO2 (M = 64 g/mol) and He (M = 4 g/mol), the comparison is √(64/4) = √16 = 4.
This is the square of the correct answer, not the result of the square root operation.
This value is a misinterpretation of the molar masses without applying the square root appropriately.

questions.explanation: According to Graham's law of effusion, the rate of diffusion of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass. Therefore, the comparative rate of diffusion can be calculated using the formula: Rate of diffusion = √(M1/M2) Here, M1 is the molar mass of SO2 (64 g/mol), and M2 is the molar mass of He (4 g/mol). Plugging in these values gives: √(64/4) = √16 = 4. Therefore, the comparative rate of diffusion of helium to sulfur dioxide is 4. The incorrect options arise from not correctly applying the square root to the ratio of molar masses.

questions.text: The unit of viscosity is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Joule is a unit of energy or work, not viscosity. It measures the amount of work done when a force is applied over a distance.
N/m2, or pascal (Pa), is a unit of pressure, not viscosity. It represents force per unit area.
Dynes per centimeter is a unit of surface tension, not viscosity. It measures force along a line of unit length.
Poise is the unit of viscosity in the centimetre-gram-second (cgs) system. It quantifies the internal resistance of fluid to flow.
The erg is a unit of energy in the cgs system, not viscosity. It is a small unit of work or energy.

questions.explanation: The cgs unit of viscosity is poise (P) whereas the SI unit of viscosity is newton-second per square meter, which is usually expressed as pascal-second.

questions.text: Glass is a/an:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Glass isn’t a pure solid because it lacks the ordered arrangement.

Glass is an amorphous solid / non-crystalline solid in which the atoms or molecules aren’t arranged in a definite lattice pattern. Amorphous solids are also known as super-cooled liquids. Glass has a chemical formula of SiO2.
It isn’t a mixture nor does it contain sodium and calcium.
It isn’t crystalline nor made up of sodium carbonate.
An alloy is a substance that combines more than one metal or mixes metal with a non-metal.

questions.explanation: Glass is sometimes called a supercooled liquid because it does not form a crystalline structure, but instead forms an amorphous solid that allows molecules in the material to continue to move, while it is also true that it’s a homogeneous mixture of sodium and calcium silicates.

generally gas behaves more like an ideal gas at higher temperature and low pressure, as P.E. due to intermolecular forces become less significant compared with particle kinetic energy. Real gas behaves like an ideal gas at high temperatures and low pressure.
Generally gas behaves more like an ideal gas at higher temperature and low pressure.

questions.explanation: Generally gas behaves more like an ideal gas at higher temperature and low pressure, as P.E due to intermolecular forces become less significant compared with particle kinetic energy. Real gas behaves like an ideal gas at high temperatures and low pressure.

questions.text: An example of a solution not in equilibrium is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Incorrect. A chemical pH indicator changes color based on the pH of the solution as it reaches different equilibria states. The indicator itself doesn't disturb the equilibrium of the solution it's in.
Incorrect. An acid-base buffer is designed to maintain equilibrium by neutralizing added acids or bases to resist pH changes, effectively maintaining a stable equilibrium condition.
Incorrect. An anhydrous solution simply lacks water. Whether it is in equilibrium depends on the components and conditions, and being anhydrous does not inherently mean it is out of equilibrium.

Correct. A hypotonic solution can lead to water movement across semipermeable membranes, such as into cells, causing an osmotic imbalance. This movement indicates that the system is initially not in equilibrium until the water movement ceases.

questions.explanation: A hypotonic solution can cause water to move across a semipermeable membrane (like into a cell), creating an osmotic imbalance. This movement continues until equilibrium is reached, so initially, it is not in equilibrium

questions.text: The internal energy of an ideal gas depends upon its:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The internal energy of an ideal gas is not affected by molecular size because it is related to molecular motion, not size. In ideal gases, molecules are considered point particles.
Pressure does not directly determine the internal energy of an ideal gas. Instead, it's an outcome of molecular collisions and temperature, which is the actual determinant of internal energy.
Volume does not affect the internal energy of an ideal gas. Internal energy is an extensive property and is determined by temperature, not the space the gas occupies.
This is correct. For an ideal gas, the internal energy is primarily associated with the translational kinetic energy of its molecules, which is directly proportional to temperature.

questions.explanation: In an ideal gas, the inter-molecular collisions are assumed to be absent and the collisions are perfectly elastic. Thus, the gas possesses only translational kinetic energy and hence the internal energy of the ideal gas depends only on temperature.

The density of an ideal gas depends on the molecular weight, temperature, and pressure of the system.

questions.text: CO2 and SO2 both are tri-atomic molecules but the heat of vaporization of SO2 is greater than that of CO2 due to the:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Electronegativity is a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons in a chemical bond. While sulfur (S) is more electronegative than carbon (C), it doesn't directly explain the difference in heat of vaporization between SO2 and CO2.

The size of the molecules is not a decisive factor in determining the heat of vaporization. While SO2 is larger than CO2 due to the presence of an additional oxygen atom, it is the molecular interactions (dipole-dipole forces) that play a more significant role in heat of vaporization.

options.text: SO2 is polar and CO2 is non-polar
options.explanation: SO2 is polar, and CO2 is nonpolar, resulting in stronger intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole interactions) in SO2 and a higher heat of vaporization compared to CO2.

options.text: SO2 is more acidic than CO2
options.explanation: Acidity is related to the ability of a substance to donate a proton (H+ ion). While SO2 can act as an acid in certain reactions, this property doesn't directly explain the difference in heat of vaporization. The heat of vaporization is primarily influenced by intermolecular forces, as discussed earlier.

questions.explanation: More polar molecules tend to form strong intermolecular forces which require a significant amount of heat to be overcome hence SO2 has a greater latent heat of vaporization. The polarity difference between the two molecules is explained below:CO2 is a linear molecule so the resultant dipole moment is zero as the individual dipole moments of the two CO bonds cancel each other out. SO2, on the other hand, is an angular molecule due to which it has a net dipole moment hence SO2 is polar and CO2 is non-polar.

questions.text: Which of the following substances has a giant covalent structure?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Iodine crystal is composed of diatomic molecules (I2) held together by weak van der Waals forces. It does not have a giant covalent structure.
Solid carbon dioxide, also known as dry ice, consists of discrete CO2 molecules held together by weak van der Waals forces. It is not a giant covalent structure; rather, it is a molecular solid.

Silica, also known as silicon dioxide (SiO2), has a giant covalent structure. In a giant covalent structure, atoms are bonded together by strong covalent bonds throughout the entire structure, forming a network. Silica is an example of a network covalent solid, where each silicon atom is covalently bonded to four oxygen atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, and this network extends throughout the entire crystal. The strong covalent bonds result in a high melting point and a hard, crystalline structure.

White phosphorus (P4) exists as P4 tetrahedra, where each phosphorus atom is covalently bonded to three other phosphorus atoms. However, it does not form a giant covalent structure like silica. White phosphorus has a molecular structure at the P4 level.

questions.explanation: Silica tends to form long-chain covalent structures such as carbon so it has a giant covalent structure. Diamond and graphite (forms of carbon) and silicon dioxide (silica) are examples of giant covalent structures (lattices) of atoms. All the atoms in these structures are linked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds and so they have very high melting points.

questions.text: For a given mass of gas, if the pressure is reduced to half and its temperature is doubled, then volume V will become?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Correct. Using the ideal gas law, doubling the temperature and halving the pressure results in the volume being quadrupled.
Incorrect. This option assumes a direct relationship with temperature change without considering the effect of reduced pressure.
Incorrect. This option suggests the volume decreases, which contradicts the effects of increasing temperature and decreasing pressure.
Incorrect. This option overestimates the volume change by assuming both temperature and pressure changes have a multiplicative effect rather than a balanced one.

questions.explanation: To determine how the volume changes, we use the ideal gas law: PV = nRT. Since the number of moles (n) and the gas constant (R) remain constant, we can compare the initial and final states of the gas:P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2.Given that the pressure is halved (P2 = P1/2) and the temperature is doubled (T2 = 2T1), substitute these values into the equation:(P1V1/T1) = ((P1/2) * V2) / (2T1)Rearranging and simplifying gives V2 = 4V1, indicating that the volume becomes four times the original.Therefore, the correct answer is 4V. Other options incorrectly interpret the effects of these changes on the volume.

questions.text: What assumptions are made in the Kinetic Theory about an ideal gas? There are no forces of attraction between molecules The molecules are in a state of continuous random motionThe size of the molecule is negligible
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Statement I is correct, but it is not the only correct statement. Other assumptions are also part of the Kinetic Theory for ideal gases.
Statement II is correct, but it is not the only correct statement. Other assumptions are also part of the Kinetic Theory for ideal gases.
Statements I and II are correct, but statement III is also a valid assumption in the Kinetic Theory for ideal gases.
Statements II and III are correct, but statement I is also a valid assumption in the Kinetic Theory for ideal gases.
All three statements are correct, as they collectively represent the assumptions of the Kinetic Molecular Theory for an ideal gas.

questions.explanation: The Kinetic Molecular Theory assumes that for an ideal gas, there are no forces of attraction between molecules (Statement I), molecules are in a state of continuous random motion (Statement II), and the size of the molecules is negligible compared to the distances between them (Statement III). These assumptions help explain the behavior of gases in terms of pressure, volume, and temperature relationships. Options A, B, C, and D are incorrect because they do not encompass all these fundamental assumptions.

questions.text: What is the temperature at which the volume of gas is said to be zero?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At 0°C or 273 K, gases still occupy a significant volume, specifically 22.414 dm³ for an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure. This is not the temperature where the volume is zero.
At 4°C, water reaches its maximum density, but gases do not have zero volume. This temperature is unrelated to gases reaching zero volume.
This is the theoretical temperature known as absolute zero, where a gas would have zero volume according to the ideal gas law. At this point, molecular motion effectively stops.
At -173.16°C, gases still have volume, and this temperature is far above absolute zero. At such temperatures, some gases may become liquids, but they do not have zero volume.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is -273.15°C, which is the theoretical point known as absolute zero. At absolute zero, the kinetic energy of particles is minimal, and according to the ideal gas law, the volume of a gas would be zero. This is a theoretical limit because reaching absolute zero is not practically possible. Other temperatures, such as 0°C or 4°C, are not applicable here as gases do not reach zero volume at these points. -173.16°C is also incorrect because it is above absolute zero, and gases may even condense into liquids at such temperatures, but they do not have zero volume.

questions.text: Matter changes from one state to another state on change in _.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Density is a property that refers to the mass per unit volume of a substance and does not directly cause a change in the state of matter.
Pressure can change the state of matter. For example, increasing pressure can transform a gas into a liquid and subsequently into a solid by bringing particles closer together.
Viscosity describes a fluid's resistance to flow and does not directly affect the state of matter.
Not all these factors directly change the state of matter; only pressure and temperature do.

questions.explanation: The state of matter changes primarily due to variations in temperature and pressure. For instance, when solids are heated, they can melt into liquids, and liquids can vaporize into gases with further heating. Conversely, increasing pressure can compress gases into liquids and solids by reducing the space between particles. Therefore, pressure is a key factor in changing states of matter. Density and viscosity are properties of matter but they do not directly change its state.

questions.text: The Unit cell with crystallographic dimensions a=b≠c , α=β=γ=90 degrees is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is not correct. A cubic unit cell has three equal dimensions (a = b = c) and all angles equal to 90 degrees (α = β = γ = 90°).
A tetragonal unit cell has two equal dimensions (a = b ≠ c) and all angles equal to 90 degrees (α = β = γ = 90°).
This option is also incorrect. A Monoclinic unit cell has three unequal dimensions (a ≠ b ≠ c) and one angle (β) that is not equal to 90 degrees (α = γ = 90°).

This option is also incorrect. A hexagonal unit cell has two equal dimensions (a = b ≠ c) and all angles equal to 90 degrees (α = β = 90°) but has a third axis that is perpendicular to the plane of the other two axes and is longer than them.

questions.explanation: A tetragonal unit cell has two equal dimensions (a = b ≠ c) and all angles equal to 90 degrees (α = β = γ = 90°).

questions.text: The compression factor (compressibility factor) for one mole of a Van der Waals gas at 00 C and 100 atmosphere pressure is found to be 0.5. Assuming that the volume of the gas molecule is negligible, calculate the Van der Waals constant 'a'.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Based on the information provided, using the van der Waals equation:Z = PV/RT = 0.5where P = 100 atm, V = 1 mol, R = 0.08206 L.atm/mol.K, and T = 273 K.Rearranging the equation to solve for the van der Waals constant "a":a = (27/64) * (RTc)^2 / Pcwhere Tc and Pc are the critical temperature and pressure for the gas, respectively.Assuming the gas in question is a non-polar gas, the critical compressibility factor Zc for such gases is approximately 0.3. From this, we can solve for the critical pressure:Zc = 3/8 * (Pc/RTc)0.3 = 3/8 * (Pc/RTc)Pc/RTc = 0.8Solving for a:a = (27/64) * (RTc)^2 / (0.8Pc)Assuming Tc = 190 K and Pc = 56 atm (typical values for non-polar gases), we get:a = (27/64) * (0.08206 L.atm/mol.K * 190 K)^2 / (0.8 * 56 atm) = 1.254 L^2.atm/mol^2Therefore, the value of a is 1.254 L^2.atm/mol^2.
According to the solution, this is not the correct answer.

questions.text: Which of the following gases is more ideal at STP?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Sulfur dioxide has polar covalent bonds, and it is a more complex molecule than hydrogen. It has stronger intermolecular forces compared to hydrogen gas, making it less ideal.
Ammonia has polar covalent bonds, and like SO2, it has stronger intermolecular forces compared to H2.

H2 is more ideal at STP because it follows the ideal gas law more closely than the other gases listed. The ideal gas law, PV=nRT, describes the behavior of ideal gases under certain conditions. At STP (standard temperature and pressure), which is 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm, the volume of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the number of moles of gas present. Since H2 is a very small molecule with low intermolecular forces, it has a high degree of freedom of movement and easily escapes the attractive forces between its molecules, making it behave more ideally than the other gases listed.
Hydrogen sulfide is also a more complex molecule than H2 and has polar covalent bonds. It experiences stronger intermolecular forces than H2.

questions.explanation: In order to understand the answer to this question, we first need to address the definition of an ideal gas:Hydrogen is the closest to ideal gases because it has the least amount of excluded volume (thereby bringing its molar volume close to that of an ideal gas), and the weakest intermolecular attractions. (Refer to bullet points 1 and 2 above)Bear in mind that the smaller the atomic number, the weaker the London dispersion forces, and by extension the intermolecular interactions.H2 has the smallest molecular mass amongst the options given.

questions.text: How many balloons of capacity 0.25dm3 at 1atm pressure can be filled from a hydrogen gas cylinder of 5dm3 capacity at 10 atm?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Since the answer is 200, this option is incorrect.
Since the answer is 200, this option is incorrect.
Since the answer is 200, this option is incorrect.

The ideal gas law can be used to solve this problem. The equation is: PV = nRT Where: P = pressure (in atm) V = volume (in liters) n = number of moles R = ideal gas constant (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K) T = temperature (in Kelvin)First, we need to find the number of moles of hydrogen gas in the cylinder. We can use the following equation:PV = nRTn = PV/RTn = (5 dm3 x 10atm) / (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K x 293 K)n = 202.5 molesNext, we need to find the number of moles of hydrogen gas required to fill one balloon:n = PV/RTn = (0.25 dm3 x 1 atm) / (0.0821 L.atm/mol.K x 293 K)n = 0.0100 molesFinally, we can divide the total number of moles of hydrogen gas in the cylinder by the number of moles required for each balloon:202.5 moles / 0.0100 moles per balloon = 2025 balloonsTherefore, the answer is 200, which is the closest option.

questions.explanation: We will use Boyle's law for the relationship between volume and pressure. PV = k Where : P = Pressure V = Volume k = Constant Lets get PV for each : Balloon : 0.25 × 1 = 0.25...... k₁ Gas cylinder : 5 × 10 = 50......... k₂ To get the number n of the balloons the cylinder can supply we get : k₂ / k₁ = n 50 / 0.25 = 200 The answer is 200 balloons.

questions.text: The expression PV=nRT represents the:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, the total pressure by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of each of the constituent gases. Where P total = P1+ P2+ P3+…..Pn.
Avogadro's Law is stated mathematically as follows: Vn=k, where V is the volume of the gas, n is the number of moles of the gas, and k is a proportionality constant.

The empirical relationships among the volume, the temperature, the pressure, and the amount of a gas can be combined into the ideal gas law, PV=nRT. This law is the generalized form of Boyles, Charles, and Avogadro’s law. Hence, this is the correct option.

Van der Waals equation is an equation that relates the temperature, pressure, and volume of a non-ideal gas and takes into account both the intermolecular forces between the gas molecules and the volume of the gas molecules themselves. P = [RT/ V-b] – [a/V^2]

questions.explanation: PV=nRT is a General gas equation for an ideal gas equation. Avagadro’s law is V1n2=V2n1, Dalton’s law is Ptotal=P1+P2+P3…, Van der Waal equation is (P+V2an2)(V−nb)=nRT.

questions.text: Rate of effusion of a gas depends upon:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The rate of effusion depends on the size of the pinhole as the larger the size, the greater number of molecules will be allowed to escape and the greater will be the rate.

The rate of effusion is directly proportional to the pressure according to Graham's law. As greater the pressure of the gaseous molecules, the more rapid their collision with the wall of the container and the more rapidly they will effuse out.
The R.O.E also depends on the temperature. As more T will provide more K.E to the molecules the faster they will move and effuse.
The R.O.E also depends on the molecular mass. It is inversely proportional to the mass according to Graham's law of effusion.
The rate of effusion depends upon all of the above characters. Thus, this is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Effusion: When gas is confined to a container at higher pressure than in the surrounding atmosphere it will escape from a small hole that is opened in the container until the pressure outside and inside has been equalized. This process is called effusion. Graham's law of diffusion states "under the same conditions of temperature and pressure, the rates of diffusion of different gases are inversely proportional to the square roots of their molecular masses or their densities."

questions.text: Upon which factor is vapor pressure not dependent:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The greater the temperature, the more molecules will gain sufficient K.E and hence, the greater the vapor pressure. Thus, the V.P of a liquid is dependent on the temperature.

The stronger the intermolecular forces, the lesser will be the V.P as the molecules will be tightly bound to each other and won’t be able to escape easily. Hence, the V.P is also inversely dependent on the intermolecular forces.
As the density increases the pressure also increases due to a decrease in the volume of the liquid. Hence, V.P is also dependent on the density of the liquid.

The V.P. of a liquid is independent of the surface area of the liquid. This is because the rate of evaporation is balanced with the rate of condensation. And any increase in the surface area will not be sufficient to increase the V.P until the molecules don’t have sufficient energy of action. Hence, this is the correct option.

questions.explanation: The surface area of liquid or solid in contact does not affect vapor pressure. Other factors do have an impact on vapor pressure. Higher density decreases vapor pressure, higher temperature increases vapor pressure and higher intermolecular forces also decrease the vapor pressure.

questions.text: At the transition temperature of a crystalline solid, the substance exists:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At the transition temperature of a crystalline solid, the substance is not in the most stable geometric form. Rather it is in transition i.e. in between two stable forms. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The transition temperature only exists between two crystalline solid states and not liquid states.

options.text: In dynamic equilibrium between two crystalline forms
options.explanation: The transition temperature is the temperature at which a material changes from one crystal state (allotrope) to another. More formally, it is the temperature at which two crystalline forms of a substance can co-exist in equilibrium. Hence, this option is correct.

options.text: In one solid geometrical form only
options.explanation: The word transition shows that it is something in between, which can only exist in two. Hence, it does not apply to a single solid geometric form.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because, from the thermodynamic standpoint, the transition temperature can be characterized by the temperature, T0, at which two crystalline phases can coexist.

questions.text: At 1489 mm of Hg, water will boil at:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Although you are not expected to know the calculation of boiling points at various pressures, you must understand that at pressures higher than atmospheric pressure, the boiling point must also be higher than that at atmospheric pressure. This is because the boiling point is defined as the temperature at which the vapor pressure equals the external pressure. So, the higher the pressure, the higher the required temperature to evaporate enough liquid to equal the external pressure. Since 1489 mm of Hg>760 mm of Hg(atmospheric pressure), the boiling point must also be greater than 100. The best option in this case is, therefore, option A.
This option is incorrect. At 1489 mmHg, the boiling point of water is higher than 110°C
The boiling point of water is 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm or 760 mmHg), but at 1489 mmHg, it will be higher than 100°C.
The boiling point of water at 1489 mm Hg is higher than 90°C.

questions.explanation: At a pressure of 1489 mmHg (millimeters of mercury), water will boil at approximately 120 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit). The boiling point of a substance, such as water, depends on the pressure applied to it. Under normal atmospheric pressure at sea level, which is around 760 mmHg, water boils at 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit). However, when the pressure is increased, as in the case of 1489 mmHg, the boiling point of water also increases. At higher pressures, the boiling point of water increases because the additional pressure on the water molecules raises the energy required for them to overcome the intermolecular forces and transition from the liquid phase to the gaseous phase (vaporization). This results in an increase in the boiling point temperature. It's important to note that the boiling point of water can vary with altitude and atmospheric conditions, as the atmospheric pressure changes with elevation. Additionally, impurities and dissolved substances in the water can also affect its boiling point. The value mentioned above (100 degrees Celsius at 1489 mmHg) is an approximate estimation based on the relationship between pressure and boiling point. questions.text: Which one of the following has the highest lattice energy?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The lattice enthalpy is the enthalpy change involved in the formation of an ionic compound from gaseous ions (an exothermic process).The Lattice energy is directly proportional to the charges of the ions but inversely proportional to the radii of the ions or more generally the distance between ions. Lattice Energy α (charge of ion/radius of the ion).As we go down in a group (Group 1 and 7 in this case), ionic radius increases, and as the net charge is constant (+1 and -1 respectively), the lattice energy of their compounds decreases.Order of cation radius: K+> Na+> Li+Order of anion radius: Br-> Cl-Hence, the compound LiCl will have the greatest Lattice energy as both the ions have the least radius.Note: Across a period from left to right as the charge on ion increases, lattice energy increases.

questions.text: What is the % relative humidity at 25 oC and 0.01876 atm if the vapor pressure of water is 23.76 mm Hg?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: 40% Relative Humidity Relative Humidity = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapor / Vapor Pressure of Water) x 100 Relative Humidity = (14.268 mm Hg / 23.76 mm Hg) x 100 = 60.08% Therefore, Option A (40% Relative Humidity) is incorrect.

50% Relative Humidity Relative Humidity = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapor / Vapor Pressure of Water) x 100 Relative Humidity = (14.268 mm Hg / 23.76 mm Hg) x 100 = 60.08% Therefore, Option B (50% Relative Humidity) is incorrect.

Relative humidity is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor present in the air to the vapor pressure of water at a particular temperature, expressed as a percentage. In this question, we are given the vapor pressure of water (23.76 mm Hg) and the temperature (25 oC). We can use the formula: Relative Humidity = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapor / Vapor Pressure of Water) x 100 We need to first convert the given pressure of 0.01876 atm to mm Hg. 1 atm = 760 mm Hg, so 0.01876 atm = 14.268 mm Hg. Then, we can substitute the values in the formula: Relative Humidity = (14.268 / 23.76) x 100 = 60% Therefore, the % relative humidity at 25 oC and 0.01876 atm is 60%.

70% Relative Humidity Relative Humidity = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapor / Vapor Pressure of Water) x 100 Relative Humidity = (14.268 mm Hg / 23.76 mm Hg) x 100 = 60.08% Therefore, Option D (70% Relative Humidity) is incorrect

questions.explanation: Relative humidity is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor present in the air to the vapor pressure of water at a particular temperature, expressed as a percentage. In this question, we are given the vapor pressure of water (23.76 mm Hg) and the temperature (25 oC). We can use the formula: Relative Humidity = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapor / Vapor Pressure of Water) x 100 We need to first convert the given pressure of 0.01876 atm to mm Hg. 1 atm = 760 mm Hg, so 0.01876 atm = 14.268 mm Hg. Then, we can substitute the values in the formula: Relative Humidity = (14.268 / 23.76) x 100 = 60% Therefore, the % relative humidity at 25 oC and 0.01876 atm is 60%.

questions.text: Change in extensive property is proportional to the change in _ of material.

questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Change in temperature does not change mass or volume( extensive property).
Volume change is not the answer because they asked about the extensive property that includes all extensive properties not only volume.

The two types of physical properties of matter are intensive properties and extensive properties. Here is the definition of an extensive property in chemistry. An extensive property is a property of matter that changes as the amount of matter changes. Like other physical properties, an extensive property may be observed and measured without any chemical change (reaction) occurring.Extensive Property Examples:Mass and volume are extensive properties. As more matter is added to a system, both mass and volume change.Extensive Versus Intensive Properties:In contrast to extensive properties, intensive properties do not depend on the amount of matter in a sample. They are the same whether you're looking at a large amount of material or a tiny quantity. An example of an intensive property is electrical conductivity. The electrical conductivity of a wire depends on its composition, not the length of the wire. Density and solubility are two other examples of intensive properties.It is the right answer because changing quantity will change mass and volume (extensive properties).
Pressure doesn't change mass (extensive property).

questions.explanation: Mass and volume are examples of extensive properties. An intensive property is a property of matter that depends only on the type of matter in a sample and not on the amount. Color, temperature, and solubility are examples of intensive properties.

questions.text: Which of the following has the highest electrical conductivity?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Solution and Colloids

options.explanation: Aqueous sugar solutions generally do not conduct electricity well. While water itself can conduct electricity to some extent due to the presence of ions, sugar molecules do not dissociate into ions easily in solution. Therefore, the conductivity of an aqueous sugar solution is relatively low compared to other options.

Graphite is an allotrope of carbon and is known for its high electrical conductivity. The carbon atoms in graphite are arranged in a layered structure, forming sheets of hexagonal rings. These sheets have delocalized electrons that can move freely along the layers, allowing for efficient electron flow and high electrical conductivity. Therefore, solid graphite has a high electrical conductivity.

Solid sodium chloride, also known as table salt, is a crystalline structure composed of alternating sodium (Na+) and chloride (Cl-) ions. While ionic compounds like sodium chloride can conduct electricity when dissolved in water or molten state due to the movement of ions, they do not conduct electricity in the solid state. The fixed positions of the ions in the crystal lattice restrict the movement of charged particles, resulting in low electrical conductivity for solid sodium chloride.

Gaseous carbon dioxide consists of covalently bonded molecules. Since the molecules do not have a net charge and do not dissociate into ions in the gaseous state, gaseous carbon dioxide does not conduct electricity.

questions.explanation: Graphite is an allotrope of carbon and is known for its high electrical conductivity. The carbon atoms in graphite are arranged in a layered structure, forming sheets of hexagonal rings. These sheets have delocalized electrons that can move freely along the layers, allowing for efficient electron flow and high electrical conductivity. Therefore, solid graphite has a high electrical conductivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry

Incorrect option. Infinite dimensions would imply no boundary, which is not applicable to effusion.
Incorrect option. A slit allows multiple molecules to pass, which is more characteristic of diffusion.
Correct option. Effusion occurs through a hole that is comparable in size to individual gas molecules, allowing them to pass one at a time.

questions.explanation: Effusion involves the movement of gas molecules through a tiny hole into a vacuum or another container. For effusion to occur, the hole must be small enough that gas molecules pass through it one at a time, which is why the correct option is 'Molecular dimensions.' Larger holes would allow more molecules to pass simultaneously, resembling diffusion. 'Infinite dimensions' imply no barrier, making effusion impossible, while 'Slit-like dimensions' would still permit multiple molecules to pass at once, not aligning with the concept of effusion.

questions.text: The necessary condition for Boyle's law to hold is that the process must be _.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: An isobaric process means the pressure remains constant, which does not align with Boyle's law where the temperature must be constant.
In an adiabatic process, no heat is exchanged, but Boyle's law requires a constant temperature, not an isolated system.
Isochoric means the volume is constant, but Boyle's law describes the inverse relationship between pressure and volume when temperature is constant.
Isothermal processes occur at a constant temperature, which is the necessary condition for Boyle's law to apply.

questions.explanation: Boyle's law states that the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume when the temperature is kept constant. This is why the process must be isothermal for Boyle's law to hold. In contrast, an isobaric process keeps pressure constant, an adiabatic process involves no heat transfer, and an isochoric process maintains constant volume, none of which satisfy the conditions of Boyle's law.

questions.text: When animals inhale air, oxygen will move into the lungs due to the difference in partial pressure of oxygen in the lungs and atmosphere. This obeys
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Pascal's Law is more related to the behavior of fluids (liquids and gases) in confined spaces. It states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to all portions of the fluid and the walls of its container. It's not directly applicable to the movement of gases during inhalation.

Dalton's Law states that the total pressure exerted by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of individual gases. In the context of breathing, as animals inhale, the partial pressure of oxygen in the lungs decreases, creating a pressure gradient that causes oxygen to move from the higher partial pressure in the atmosphere to the lower partial pressure in the lungs.

Charles's Law describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, assuming constant pressure. It states that, at constant pressure, the volume of a given amount of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature. Charles's Law is not directly related to the movement of gases during inhalation.

Bohr's Law is not a recognized scientific law in the context of gas behavior or respiratory processes. Niels Bohr made significant contributions to atomic theory, particularly the structure of the atom, but he didn't formulate a law related to the movement of gases during breathing.

questions.explanation: When animals inhale air, oxygen will move into the lungs due to the difference in partial pressure of oxygen in the lungs and atmosphere. This obeys DALTON’S LAW. Dalton's law (also called Dalton's law of partial pressures) states that in a mixture of non-reacting gases, the total pressure exerted is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases.

This option suggests that the densities of liquids are greater than gases and far away from solids. However, the correct relationship is that the densities of liquids are greater than gases but closer to solids.

questions.explanation: The densities of liquids are much greater than gases but closer to solids. So the density of solids is generally more than that of liquids and gases.

In the context of liquid crystals, the transition between the ordered liquid crystalline state and the isotropic liquid state is often referred to as the "clearing" temperature. So, a liquid crystalline state exists between the melting temperature and the clearing temperature.

While melting temperature is mentioned in the question, boiling and freezing temperatures are not directly associated with the transition between liquid crystal and isotropic liquid states. Therefore, "all of these" is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: So, a liquid crystalline state exists between two temperatures i.e. melting temperature and clearing temperature. questions.text: The nature of binding force in ionic crystals is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: While some materials may exhibit magnetic properties, the primary force holding ionic crystals together is electrostatic attraction between ions. Magnetic forces are associated with the alignment of magnetic dipoles and are not the primary force in ionic crystals.

Ionic crystals are formed through the attraction between oppositely charged ions. While there may be repulsive forces between ions of the same charge, the overall force holding the crystal together is attractive (electrostatic).

Ionic crystals are held together by electrostatic forces of attraction between positively charged ions (cations) and negatively charged ions (anions). This electrostatic force is often referred to as ionic bonding. The strong attraction between opposite charges leads to the formation of a stable crystal lattice structure in ionic compounds.

The forces in ionic crystals are not generally considered weak; in fact, ionic bonds are often quite strong. The strength of the electrostatic attraction between ions in ionic crystals contributes to the stability of their lattice structures.

questions.explanation: An ionic crystal consists of ions bound together by electrostatic attraction. The arrangement of ions in a regular, geometric structure is called a crystal lattice. Examples of such crystals are the alkali halides, which include: potassium fluoride (KF).

questions.text: A fixed mass of an ideal gas is contained in a cylinder at a constant temperature. Now, the pressure of the gas is decreased. What happened to the molecules of gas?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is not necessarily true. In ideal gases, the mean square speed is related to temperature, and keeping the temperature constant, a decrease in pressure does not directly affect the mean square speed.

options.text: Number of collision between molecules and walls of container decreased
options.explanation: When the pressure of a fixed mass of an ideal gas is decreased while keeping the temperature constant, the molecules of the gas will spread out and occupy a larger volume. This is known as expansion.In an ideal gas, the behavior of the gas molecules is governed by the ideal gas law, which states:PV = nRTwhere:P = Pressure of the gasV = Volume of the gasn = Number of moles of the gasR = Gas constantT = Temperature of the gasWhen the pressure is decreased, the volume of the gas will increase to maintain the constant temperature. This occurs because the gas molecules have more space to move around, and they spread out to fill the available volume. The average kinetic energy of the gas molecules remains constant, as the temperature is kept constant. As the gas expands, the average distance between gas molecules increases, and the gas becomes less dense. The pressure decrease allows the gas to occupy a larger space while maintaining a constant temperature. This behavior is consistent with the ideal gas law and the concept of how gases behave under changes in pressure, volume, and temperature.

options.text: The force of attraction between them increase
options.explanation: In ideal gases, it is assumed that there are no intermolecular forces of attraction between gas molecules. Therefore, a decrease in pressure would not imply an increase in the force of attraction between the molecules.
Changes in pressure do not affect the size of individual gas molecules in an ideal gas. The size of gas molecules is considered negligible compared to the distances between them.

questions.explanation: Pressure is proportional to temperature if the number of particles and the volume of the container is constant. Because the area of the container has increased, there will be fewer of these collisions per unit area and the pressure will decrease. Note; The question does not clarify if the volume is constant or not. We mustn't assume it is constant.

questions.text: Who attributed the deviation of real gases from ideal behavior?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Boyle's law states that at constant temperature, the volume of a given amount of gas is inversely proportional to its pressure. It doesn't directly address the deviation of real gases from ideal behavior, so it is not the correct option in this context.

Charles's law states that at constant pressure, the volume of a given amount of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature. Similar to Boyle's law, Charles's law also does not directly explain the deviation of real gases from ideal behavior.

Avogadro's law states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of molecules. While this law is essential in understanding the behavior of gases, it does not provide an explanation for the deviations from ideal behavior.

Van Der Waals is a set of equations formulated by Johannes Diderik van der Waals to describe the behavior of real gases, taking into account the finite size of gas molecules and the attractive forces between them. These factors are not considered in the ideal gas law, which assumes point-like particles with no intermolecular forces.

questions.explanation: Van Der Waals modified the ideal gas law to describe the behavior of real gases by explicitly including the effects of molecular size and intermolecular forces.

questions.text: Carbon dioxide is an example of?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The statement is inaccurate as carbon dioxide (CO2) does not exist as ions linked by ionic bonds. Instead, it is a covalent compound comprised of two oxygen atoms that are covalently bonded to a central carbon atom.

The assertion is inaccurate, as metallic solids are characterized by a lattice structure comprised of metal atoms held together by metallic bonding. In contrast, carbon dioxide is classified as a molecular compound that features covalent bonds, rather than being classified as a metallic solid.

A molecular solid is defined as a solid comprised of discrete molecules that are held together by van der Waals forces. An example of such a solid is carbon dioxide (CO2), which exhibits relatively weak London Dispersion Forces. This characterization highlights the distinct properties associated with molecular solids.

Covalent Solid: This classification is not accurate, as covalent solids are typically characterized by a network of atoms interconnected by strong covalent bonds, exemplified by diamond, a crystalline form of carbon. In contrast, carbon dioxide is composed of discrete molecules rather than forming a continuous covalent network.

questions.explanation: A molecular solid is made up of discrete molecules held together by van der Waals forces. CO2 exemplifies this type of solid due to its London Dispersion Forces, making option C correct.

questions.text: Which of the following properties does not belong to Gases?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Gases do not have a definite volume. Unlike liquids and solids, the particles in a gas are widely spaced and move freely, occupying the entire container they are in. The volume of a gas can change with changes in temperature and pressure.

Gases also do not have a definite shape. The particles in a gas move randomly and spread out to fill the entire available space of the container. They do not have a fixed arrangement like the particles in solids, which have a definite shape.

Gases generally have low density compared to liquids and solids. This is because the particles in gases are spread out and have more empty space between them. As a result, gases are much less dense than liquids and solids.

This option does not belong to gases. In gases, the particles have weak interactions with each other. The attractive forces between gas particles are typically much weaker compared to those in liquids and solids. This is why gases can easily compress and expand, and their particles can move freely.

questions.explanation: Strong interactions as gases have weak interactions since the particles move quickly in random motion and move far apart from each other which results in weak intermolecular forces of attraction.

questions.text: To conduct electricity through Ionic Solids, the ions should be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In the context of ionic solids, "excited" usually refers to the state of an electron in an atom or ion that has absorbed energy and moved to a higher energy level. While the concept of electronic excitation is essential in various chemical and physical processes, it is not directly related to the conduction of electricity through ionic solids.

"Energized" generally means that a system or particle has gained energy. In the case of ionic solids, energy is indeed required to initiate the conduction of electricity, but it is not the ions themselves that need to be energized. Instead, the energy typically comes from an external source like an electric field, which allows the ions to move and conduct electricity.

The correct answer is "c) Free." For a material to conduct electricity, it must have mobile charge carriers. In ionic solids, these mobile charge carriers are free ions, which can move under the influence of an electric field. When an ionic solid is dissolved in water or melted, the ions become free and can move, resulting in electrical conductivity.

While the ions in an ionic solid do vibrate and have thermal motion, this random motion is not directly related to the conduction of electricity through the solid. Electrical conductivity in ionic solids primarily depends on the availability of free ions that can move in response to an electric field.

questions.explanation: To conduct energy through Ionic Solids, the ions should be free to move. Ionic compounds conduct electricity as liquids or when in solution since the ions are free to move. Ionic compounds do not conduct electricity in a solid state since the ions are held in place and can not move.

questions.text: The rate of diffusion of liquids is _ than gases.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The rate of diffusion of liquids is lower compared to gases. In the process of diffusion, particles move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. Gases have greater kinetic energy and are more widely spaced compared to liquids, allowing gas particles to move more freely and quickly. As a result, gases diffuse at a faster rate than liquids.
This option is incorrect. As explained above, the rate of diffusion of gases is higher than that of liquids.
This option is incorrect. The rate of diffusion of liquids is not equal to that of gases. Gases diffuse faster due to their higher kinetic energy and more significant intermolecular spaces.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Only option "a) Lower" is correct.

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the intermixing (movement) of molecules. When looking at diffusion you must look at two factors: forces of attraction and kinetic energy of molecules. Liquids have a stronger force of attraction than gases, and liquids have less KE than gases. This is because gas particles are further apart compared to liquid particles and move faster. This makes liquids have a lower rate of diffusion than gases.

questions.text: The energy is _ when oppositely charged ions are brought close to each other.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: When oppositely charged ions are brought close to each other, energy is released. This is because of the attractive electrostatic force between the opposite charges. The potential energy of the system decreases as the ions approach each other.
This option is incorrect. Energy is not absorbed but released when oppositely charged ions come close to each other.
This option is incorrect as well. The energy changes when oppositely charged ions interact. As they move closer, the potential energy decreases, leading to a change in energy.

This option is incorrect too. The energy is not constant but changes as the distance between the oppositely charged ions changes. It decreases as the ions approach each other and increase as they move apart.

questions.explanation: Oppositely charged ions form a crystalline lattice which releases lattice energy.

questions.text: What does the constant 'n' represent in the equation of state for an ideal gas PV=nRT?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: Number of atoms in the gas
options.explanation: In the ideal gas equation PV=nRT, the constant 'n' represents the number of moles of the gas, not the number of atoms. A mole is a unit of measurement used to express the amount of a substance in chemistry and is defined as 6.022 x 1023 particles, which can be atoms, molecules, or other entities depending on the context.

options.text: Number of moles of the gas
options.explanation: This is the correct interpretation. In the ideal gas equation, 'n' represents the number of moles of the gas present in the system. Moles are essential for quantifying the amount of substance in chemical reactions and thermodynamic calculations.
The term "nucleons" refers to the particles found in the atomic nucleus, which includes protons and neutrons. In the context of the ideal gas equation, 'n' does not represent the number of nucleons.

While 'n' in the ideal gas equation does involve the concept of the number of particles, it represents the number of moles of the gas rather than the specific number of molecules. One mole of any gas contains Avogadro's number (6.022 x 1023) of molecules.

questions.explanation: In the equation of state of an ideal gas PV=nRT; P is the absolute pressure of the gas in Pascals, V is the volume occupied by the gas in dm3, n is the number of moles of the gas, R is the molar gas constant (also known as the gas constant, the universal gas constant, or ideal gas constant) and T is the thermodynamic/absolute temperature in Kelvin.

questions.text: Which type of movement is shown by the atoms of the solid?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because, unlike solids, liquids have translational energy. The liquid molecules can move past each other.

This option is correct because the particles vibrate back and forth but remain in their fixed positions. As a solid is heated, the particles vibrate faster and faster until they have enough energy to break away from their fixed positions.
This option is incorrect because only gas and liquids have rotational motion.
This option is incorrect because only liquids show linear motion. After all, gases have haphazard motion.

questions.explanation: The correct option is B. Solid has a fixed shape and a fixed volume because the particles can move only a little. The particles vibrate back and forth but remain in their fixed positions. As a solid is heated, the particles vibrate faster and faster until they have enough energy to break away from their fixed positions.

This option is incorrect because it is not a correct definition of capacity.
